sentences.text: Yes, until about my third year with them.
I think in the year 1941, I married a-- a lady from Washington here.

And our marriage went along pretty well until I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went overseas, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then I began to feel that her beliefs and feelings were not sufficiently compatible with mine, [? so ?] that we should continue.
So I asked her to get a divorce, which she did.
It was in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, I can't tell you exactly.
The-- the year escapes me, but it was about the fourth year or something like that.
At all events, by-- when I got to Nuremberg, I-- by that time, I was a-- a free man.
No.
I left it in May '42 to enlist in the US Army.
I can't hear you.
Well, I knew I would be drafted, and I had wanted to get in earlier.
And I'd applied for a Navy commission, but I have a left leg that's 3/4 of an inch shorter than my right.
And although this has never stopped me from being in athletics, the Navy noticed it.
And so, all of a sudden, I find myself, despite some very good recommendations, being turned down.
And so I decided I'll go the other route, and I went in and enlisted and worked my way up, eventually was discharged as a captain.
Where was I what?
Because of my background in language and so on, I was shipped to England within three weeks.
And I expected I was going to be transferred to something where I-- my language ability and my geographic knowledge would be useful.
And instead, someone recommended me for the Inspector General's Office, and I eventually rose through the-- several ranks of sergeant to warrant officer in the first few months in England.
And we travelled all over England and Scotland and Northern Ireland investigating or in inspecting Army installations.
And I had drew up a checklist, for which I was recommended for some damn honor, in order that people, when we left, could have a listing of the kinds of things that they should be watching out for.
And we wouldn't give them to these until after we had inspected them.
And this was considered to be quite an innovative idea.
And so I got taken to North Africa by the Assistant Inspector General in England, and Colonel Tom Brand then arranged for me to get a-- I got a direct commission in North Africa.
I had to go in at the bottom.
I went in as a second lieutenant.
After the-- the Allies invaded North Africa in late November, early December 1942.
My language ability rested mainly on having had three and a half years of German at University of Wisconsin and North Central College.
But I constantly reduced the amount of interest I had in the language, and my grades fell from A to C.
So I wasn't really a very brilliant speaker in-- in German.
I could get along.
And I often had to be the interpreter in some way, particularly if the other person, the German, knew a little English between us.
So I would get along pretty well.
Now, what were your duties in North Africa?
I became the administrative officer to the Inspector General of the Mediterranean Base Section.
And I traveled with him a good deal when he made inspections and investigations, and I helped him write his reports, which were made to a General [?
Lee. ?] I forget [?
Lee's ?] first name, but a General [?
Lee. ?]
In North Africa, mainly in Algeria, but a time or two to-- to Morocco and once to Gibraltar.
Until sometime in about maybe May of 1944.
But by that time, I had been-- a friend of mine who was in the Office of Strategic Services, OSS, fellow by the name of Gerhard Van Arkel, recruited me to come with OSS.
And my main task there was training anti-Nazi Germans who had been recruited through the British Pioneer Corps.
They'd-- most of them had fought in France, and then the British had covered them and put them in the-- the British Pioneer Corps.
And they were very glad to-- to-- those that we recruited-- to serve as potential agents.
And I think out of 18 that we trained, 17 of them jumped in, parachuted in, ahead of the American troops when the American troops were already invading German territory.
Well, I was in Oran, Algeria, when I was recruited to go into OSS in Algiers, and then I stayed in Algiers during this training operation.
We had some houses, which were secret houses, both on the outskirts of Algiers and some ways out, and I worked back and forth between those houses.
No, I came back to the United States.
And they hadn't allowed me to take parachuting for fear I'd break my legs and couldn't train on other-- in other subjects.
So I came back, and I was under Arthur Goldberg, who later became a Justice of the Supreme Court.
And I said, I don't want to do any more of this without taking some parachuting myself.
So I went down to Fort Benning in May of 1944 and took a four-week course of parachuting and then came back.
And that actually led to another transfer to other kinds of work.
May and June.
The invasion of-- the Allied invasion on June 6, 1944 took place while I happened to be in Fort Benning taking parachute training.
He was head of what was called the German Labor Branch of the Office of Strategic Services.
Now, he didn't speak German himself, but he was a-- had a-- a great ability.
It was obviously shown by a lot of things he did.
And he'd been an attorney to labor unions, and so he had a sympathy for dealing with people who would be in opposition to the Nazis.

When I got back to Washington, I was walking down the street with my parachute wings on, and I ran into a man by the name of Jack Oley, who was an administrative assistant to Colonel William Brennan, who later became a Supreme Court Justice.
Now, Jack Oley had been a-- a year ahead of me at Harvard Law School.

And when he saw me on the street with these parachute bars and a bunch of stripes on my arm that I'd been overseas he says, I think we need you to help get manpower moved from the South up to the North, with the factories that are needed, to settle strikes and so on.
And we got a branch in the Army called the Labor Branch, the Army Service Forces, and I want you there.
And I said, you'll never get me.
I've just come out of parachute school.
He said, we'll see.
So Colonel Brennan called up Goldberg and persuaded him to let me go on a temporary basis.
Well, it-- it turned out to be-- it turned out to be quite a few months.
It turned out to be almost a year.
And I was still in the Labor Branch, traveling out in Wisconsin and Michigan and Illinois at the time of V-E Day.
And just before that, I had read Justice Jackson's first report to President Truman.
In the newspapers.
And then I-- using connections-- got a full copy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was so impressed by that.
And I had the impression that if I got with Justice Jackson [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and his staff, I would be able to make a contribution.
So I immediately arranged to fly back to Washington.

And with a fraternity brother who was one of the assistants to General 'Wild Bill' Donovan, I got in to see the-- another Donovan, Commander Donovan, who was the head-- the-- the legal advisor of OSS.
And within a half hour, he hired me.
He then telephoned out to-- had someone telephone out to Chicago and say that I was not going to return because the OSS had conscripted me.
Whereupon I started to study OSS documents and get ready to go to present materials to the prosecution staff.
And in various ways I did that and later got on the prosecution staff myself.
General Donovan was the head of the Office of Strategic Services.
I'd met him in Africa when I was with the Office of Strategic Services there, but it was a very casual meeting.
I was merely a lieutenant training people, and he had a lot of other more important officers than me to deal with.
So I hadn't particularly gotten to know him well.
But when I was in Nuremberg, he came over as an assistant to Justice Jackson sometime before the trial.
And he immediately grabbed a hold of me and said, I need this, I need that, and so on.
And although he was not my boss, I managed to work on the side.
My boss was Colonel Storey, the head of the Documentation Division.
And I did all this work for him, including getting translations of all the oaths which different branches of the Nazi organization had, et cetera.
But he got into a squabble with Justice Jackson.
He was preferring to hold-- have a lot of witnesses and to have some plea bargaining with some of the defendants, possible defendants, and Justice Jackson was entirely against this.
So they had a break-up, and Justice-- General Donovan just left Nuremberg, and he didn't even say good-bye.
I think he stayed until about the end of the first week of trial.
And at that time, Jackson told him that he would not allow him to cross-examine witnesses or to have witnesses, because of this dispute they had as to-- to the various things.
So it was just two great minds and two people of great stature not being able to-- to get along together.
Well, I knew that there was discrimination, and I knew that, of course, that Jews were being sent into the East.

But I certainly did not know, despite the fact that there was evidence that got back and that Roosevelt had made a-- President Roosevelt had made a statement about that Jews were being persecuted and some of them killed, it never struck me that it was at all of the degree which it later turned out to be, which we later found out to be.
I just didn't believe, or the newspapers didn't convey to me, that the persecution and extermination of Jews and gypsies was as great as it was.
I just thought it was occasional throwing people into concentration camps, isolating them, and so forth.
So when I came-- got reassigned into OSS and then could go into their-- the secret reports, I really got the story and began to see what was what.
Yes.
That would have been the end of May or the beginning of June of 1945.
OSS headquarters in Washington, DC.
Well, within a month I got myself shipped to Justice Jackson's staff and to that branch of it which was operating in Paris.
The Documentation Division was actually operating in Paris.

The first documents we had were the captured documents of the later defendant Alfred Rosenberg, who was the ideological sponsor of the Nazis from way back and who became the- the Reich Commissioner for the Ausland, for occupied Soviet Union.
And the atrocities and the slave labor and the spoliation and all the things that he was involved in were just amazing, as shown by these documents.
When I got to Paris in-- in June, Colonel Storey showed me these documents, which were lined up in two rooms.
They were so-called Leitz ordners, very thick notebooks and very orderly-- kept in very orderly fashion.

And he said to me that a few research analysts we have, which we borrowed from the-- from the American Embassy in Paris, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just are bringing me mainly things about the atrocities that are happen-- that Rosenberg was connected with, that the Nazis did.
I'd like to get something on aggressive war.
And can you find something on aggressive war in these documents?
So I went out and got myself a drink and came back.
And that evening, the first day I was in Paris with-- with OSS, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went up and I looked at these documents and I said, this is overwhelming.
But I saw one document, which was a narrow one, about half the size of the others, and it said on the outside, [GERMAN], A-P-A Norway, Foreign Political Office, Norway.

And Rosenberg, who had been the party foreign relations man-- not the state, that was von Ribbentrop and others-- would, being the party man who had liaison with pro-Nazis and-- and semi-fascists and fascists in other countries, he had this notebook on his dealings with Norway.
So I pulled it down and opened it up to just before the invasion of Norway in April or May 1940.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just before that invasion, Quisling, who was the-- who was the traitor of Norway, came down with a guy by the name of Hagelin and visited Rosenberg and was introduced to Hitler and so on.
And they'd made some notions that eventually the Germans would occupy Norway.
Of course, the Germans did shortly thereafter, and Quisling was made the approved Norwegian to run Norway under the Nazis.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The documents I found that night in Paris, just before the invasion of Norway and those afterwards, directly bore on the commission of aggressive war by the Nazis.
When Justice Jackson got the translation of these documents within the week-- he was then in London-- he was extremely pleased.
And that led to my getting-- to have something of an in with respect to finding documents, which is very funny because I was not that great a linguist or anything like that.
But you know, you-- you did the best you could.
Well, Colonel Storey had had a-- a very considerable interest-- considerable life doing special missions of one kind or another for one group or another.
He eventually became the president of the American Bar Association.
But somehow or other, OSS picked him up and wanted him to become a major deputy on the Jackson staff.
So OSS hired him.
I met him as he was going through Washington in June of 1945.
And he said, I would like you, whatever I'm going to do overseas, to be one of my assistants.
And I said, well, if you can arrange it.
So when I got, within a week or so, a notice from Paris that he wanted me to come, I was very glad, and I did go within a couple of weeks after that.
About two months.
But during that time, I flew in to Wiesbaden and Frankfurt and back to London.

And I-- because of my background in labor and because of my OSS connections, I-- I met some labor leaders who had escaped from Germany before the Nazis grabbed them and had taken up foreign residence in Great Britain.
And they told me of some underground people that had managed to survive in-- in Germany.
So when I got to Nuremberg, I already knew some anti-Nazi Germans who I was going to interrogate and make friends with and get affidavits from and so forth.
And some of the early affidavits I got from these oppositionists to the Nazi regime were all introduced in evidence, and not a one of them was ever questioned or any cross-examination done.
The evidence stood 100%.
Now, you mention that because of your discovery of the Norwegian plan documents, you came to the attention of Justice Jackson.
When did you first meet Justice Jackson?
In London in-- in June of 1945.
Well, I'd-- somebody in London said this fellow's discovered some documents and said he'd better come back here and check in with some more other-- some other people.
So I came back, and I came in to London and got to know a few of the people on the staff.
And one of them immediately said, we want to introduce you to Justice Jackson, and he's seen some of the documents you found and so on.
And so I met him, had a brief interview with him, or he questioned me a little bit and said, go back to Paris and find some more documents like this.
And that was my introduction to Justice Jackson.
I was one of the first two officers to-- to be permanently assigned to Nuremberg.
August 20, 1945, I flew into Nuremberg.
Lieutenant Colonel William H.
Baldwin.
No, he had gone in several days ahead of me.
I was the second officer to be assigned in permanently.
I think I was first lieutenant.
Yes.
And the first task I had in Nuremberg may interest you.
Baldwin immediately said to me, look, we're got a lot of documents that are laying here.
We want to get at them soon.
But the first thing we have to do is straighten up the rooms, which most of the windows of the Palace of Justice had been blown in by bombing or shelling.
And as a consequence, there was a tremendous amount of books on the floor and places-- there was one part of the building had received a direct bomb hit, but that didn't affect 9/10 of the building.

But the windows were out, and we had to fly to Paris and get windows that the Belgians had made or that someone had kept underground and have them flown back in order to put windows in-- a lot of the windows of the Palace of Justice, which became the headquarters for the trials.
At first, we went to the Grand Hotel, which it was later mainly the place for VIPs that were passing through Nuremberg.
But I didn't stay there more than about a week, and I was transferred out to Schlegelstrasse.

And Bill Baldwin, Colonel Baldwin, had a-- a residence out there, and about four or five of us were made a part of that or lived there while we traveled back and forth each morning by Army car to the Nuremberg Courthouse.
There was a Lieutenant Reitzer, R-E-I-T-Z-E-R, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another major whose name I forget, who didn't get along very well with other people there and soon left.
But I can't remember his name, and I would just as soon not give it to you anyway.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] After the trial got under way-- I'd been promoted to captain-- and I was assigned my own house and had other people living with me, so to speak.
One was Jan Sarmas, who was a-- a refugee Czech that the Americans picked up from the Czech delegation.
And he later became an American, became an American professor, partly because of Colonel Storey, who-- who liked him very much.
And he became a professor, first in Puerto Rico, then in Louisiana, and then in Southern Methodist University in-- in Texas.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were very interesting people that you met all the way along as-- as you were doing your work in Nuremberg.
Well, let's see.
I was a captain before the trial began.
And the trial began on November 20, and I was a captain by then.
So Colonel Storey had arranged this.
And one day he called me in.
And usually I'd attend a staff conference or talk about some documents that had been found and what the labor case was and the case against the German Labor Front and so on.
This one day I came in, and there was a circle of couple of colonels and himself and he said, sit down.
And I did, and I looked around, seemed funny.
And he came up, and he took off my bars.
And I thought for a moment I was going to be demoted or something, and he just pinned the captain bars on.
And that's how I knew I was being made a captain.
[LAUGHS]
